text: When an app says it collects "device information," what does that usually include?
options: OS, browser, screen size, language, and more

text: What's the average time most people spend reading terms of service?
Which of these can a website usually learn about you without asking?

options: You understood what you agreed to before agreeing
The company posted a privacy policy somewhere
Your data will never be sold

text: Behavioral data (how fast you answer, how long you linger) is useful to companies because:
options: It's required by law to collect
It reveals patterns about you beyond what you type
It can't be linked back to individuals
A design that nudges you into choices you might not want
A bug that crashes the app

text: Why do free apps often feel so personalized?
options: Your data is the product — profiles drive ads and recommendations
Phones come pre-loaded with your preferences

text: If a quiz logs how long you sat on the join screen, that's an example of:
The most ethical way to handle demo/session data after a talk is:
options: Delete it once the learning goal is done

text: What's one practical habit that actually helps with data ethics day-to-day?
options: Pause for 5 seconds and skim what you're agreeing to
Trust every "we care about privacy" banner
Share your location with every app
